title: Sh. Jaspal Singh vs Sh. Didar Singh on 16 April, 2015
body: Sh. Jaspal Singh vs Sh. Didar Singh on 16 April, 2015 IN THE COURT OF Dr. KAMINI LAU: ADDL. DISTRICT JUDGE­II (CENTRAL DISTRICT): TIS HAZARI COURTS: DELHI RCA No. 71/2013 Unique Case ID No.: 02401C0372422011 Sh. Jaspal Singh S/o Late Sh. Darshan Singh R/o F­18A & B, Moti Nagar, New Delhi ............. Appellant Versus 1. Sh. Didar Singh (since deceased now represented by) a) Smt. Avinash Kaur (widow) b) Bhajinder Singh (son) Both R/o D­22, Moti Nagar, New Delhi - 15 c) Smt. Chandeep Kaur (married daughter) W/o Maninder Singh R/o B­3, Fern Hill Apartments Door No.1, Raja Rathnam Street, Kilpauk, Chennai - 600010 d) Smt. Harneet Kaur (married daughter) W/o Hardeep Singh R/o A­37, Ashok Vihar, Phase­11, Delhi - 52 Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 1 of 22 2. Sh. Surinder Singh S/o Late Sant Singh R/o F­18A & B, Moti Nagar, New Delhi ............ Respondents Date of Institution: 17.8.2011 Arguments heard on: 16.4.2015 Date of Decision: 16.4.2015 JUDGMENT (Oral): (1) This appeal has been filed by the appellant Jaspal Singh against the order of the Ld. Trial Court dated 9.6.2011 passed by Ms. Aanchal, Ld. Civil Judge-Cum-Additional Rent Controller (North), Delhi thereby allowing the application of the respondents (defendants before the Ld. Trial Court) under Section 11 read with Section 151 CPC holding that the suit filed by the appellant (plaintiff before the Ld. Trial Court) was barred by the principles of res­judicata. (2) Various grounds have been raised in the appeal which are not being repeated for the sake of brevity. Ld. Counsel for the appellant has vehemently argued that the order of the Ld. Trial Court is patently illegal against law and fact and material available on record and is highly erroneous resulting into miscarriage of justice. It is submitted that the Ld. Trial Court has misconstrued the meaning of res­judicata and on careful reading of the provisions as laid down under Section 11 CPC it is discernible that in order to constitute res­judicata the ingredients laid therein must be satisfied which Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 2 of 22 incidentally the respondent has not been able to satisfy. It is argued that the Ld. Trial Court has committed a grave error in not appreciating the settled law that even where the parties in two suits are the same and the matter in issue is directly and substantially identical, still the conditions of Section 11 CPC are to be satisfied and proved by the party who alleged that the suit is barred by the principles of res­judicata. It is submitted that the Ld. Trial Court has wrongly presumed that the suit is barred on the principles of res­ judicata in view of the earlier orders passed by the various courts and the Ld. Trial Court had committed grave error in dismissing of the suit on the said presumption. It is also submitted that the Ld. Trial Court has failed to appreciate that the evidence on merits had already commenced and therefore the Ld. Trial Court was under bounden duty to return a finding on all the issues as per the dictum of Order XX CPC . It is further pleaded that the impugned order is self contradictory and the Ld. Trial Court has totally overlooked the fact that the subject matter of the various suits were different and under no circumstances the issue can be decided in the manner in which it has been done. It is submitted that no issue with regard to the res­judicata was pleaded in the written statement by the respondent no.1 and hence without framing the issues and giving an opportunity to the parties to lead evidence therefore, it cannot be presumed that the suit is barred by the principle of res­judicata. (3) Sh. Maninderjeet Singh Advocate for the appellant has pointed out that the father of the appellant and respondents No.2 to 5 filed a suit for Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 3 of 22 possession, damages/ mesne profits for illegal use and occupation of the premises bearing No.F­18, A & B Moti Nagar, New Delhi against the Respondent No.1, in which case issues were framed and the statement of the predecessor in interest of the Appellant was recorded who was partly cross examined. He has argued that during the course of cross­examination of the predecessor in interest of the appellant in the year 1997, the respondent No.1 moved an application under Order VI Rule 17 CPC wherein it was alleged in para No.6 that the subject matter in dispute and the matter in issue in another suit entitled as Didar Singh Vs. Darshan Singh & Others is substantially the same and that the decision in the aforesaid suit would operate as res­judicata in this suit and in para No.7 of the said application it was averred that by inadvertence, the said objection of res­judicata was not taken by the respondent no.1 (defendant before the Ld. Trial Court) and a separate application under Section 10 CPC for staying the proceedings was also moved. He has further argued that both the applications were dismissed by the Ld. Trial Court vide order dated 14.01.1998 after which the Respondent No.1 challenged the aforesaid order before the Hon'ble High Court by filing Civil Revision Petition bearing No. CR.301/98 on 18.03.1999 which was dismissed by Hon'ble Mr. Justice Vijender Jain but thereafter no further proceedings were preferred by the Respondent No.1 and therefore, the order dated 14.01.1998 attained finality. (4) It is further submitted that in the meanwhile, the LRs of Late Darshan Singh filed a suit for Declaration to the effect that the judgment and Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 4 of 22 decree passed by Sh. Sanjeev Jain the then Civil Judge, Delhi in suit under the title 'Didar Singh Versus Darshan Singh & Anr.' be declared as null and void and that the said decreed has been obtained by playing fraud and concealment of material facts and other reliefs including the relief of possession etc. was also prayed. He has pointed out that the said suit was filed in the court of Sh. Rajeev Mehra the then ADJ, Delhi who vide order dated 23.10.2007 rejected the plaint of the plaintiffs under Order VII Rule 11 CPC and being hit by Section 11 C.P.C. Ld. Counsel has further argued that the predecessor in interest had initiated proceedings before Delhi High Court on which enquiry proceeding were initiated against Sh. Sanjeev Jain, the then Civil Judge, Delhi and his judgments were under scrutiny, by the Delhi High Court and the enquiry report of the aforesaid enquiry proceedings was retained by the High Court. (5) Ld. Counsel has argued that an order under Order VII Rule 11 CPC by its very nature is not an order which can operate as res­judicata and Order VII Rule 13 CPC provides that in the event of a plaint being rejected under Order VII Rule 11 CPC , the plaintiff can file a fresh Suit on the same cause of action and hence it would be without base or conviction to say that an order under order 7 rule 11 C.P.C . can ever operate as res­judicata which order is liable to be set aside and the matter be remanded back to the Ld. Trial Court. It is submitted that the nature of an order under Order VII Rule 11 CPC means that no suit has been filed and the same is lend credence by the provisions of Order VII Rule 13 CPC and hence it cannot be said that Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 5 of 22 there was any adjudication on merits while passing the order under Order VII Rule 11 CPC and therefore any findings given on merits in the order under Order VII Rule 11 CPC are merely obiter and do not support the plea of res­juidcata either in favour of or against a party. In this regard Ld. Counsel for the appellant has placed his reliance on the judgment in the case of Shankar Lal Vs. Hira Lal reported in AIR (87) 1950 Privy Council 80. (6) Ld. Counsel has further pointed out that the Respondent No.1 under the said order dated 23.10.2007 passed by Sh. Rajeev Mehra, filed an application under Section 11 CPC in the court of Sh. Raj Kumar the then Ld. Civil Judge, Delhi in proceedings where the earlier application of the Respondent No.1 under Order VI Rule 17 CPC seeking same relief was dismissed by Sh. M.C. Gupta the then Civil Judge Delhi on 14.01.1998, of which the Civil Revision No.301/1998 was also dismissed by the Hon'ble High Court. He has argued that the order dated 23.10.2007 passed by Sh. Rajeev Mehra the then ADJ, Delhi is a judgment in­curiam and in proceedings before the said court, the judgment and decree passed by Sh. Sanjeev Jain the then Ld. Civil Judge, Delhi was assailed and therefore, by no stretch of imagination , the said judgment and decree which had been challenged in the said suit, could be held as operating as resjuidcata in the same suit. It is argued that the proceedings before Sh. Rajeev Mehra, the then ADJ, Delhi the pleading in previous suit were not filed on record and therefore question of res­judicata could not even otherwise have been decided by the said court. In this regard Ld. Counsel has placed his reliance Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 6 of 22 on the judgment in the case of A.M.K. Mariam Bibi & Ors. Vs. M.a. Abdul Rahim & Ors. reported in 2000 AIHC 661. (7) It is also argued that since the plea of res­judicata had already been negatived by order dated 14.01.1998 passed by Sh. M.C. Gupta the then Ld. Civil Judge and the said order having been upheld by the Hon'ble High Court vide order dated 18.03.1999, the second application under Section 11 CPC which has been allowed by the Ld. Trial Court, being based on the same order passed by Sh. Sanjeev Jain the then Civil Judge, Delhi, is itself barred by res­judicata and therefore, could not have been filed much less allowed. Ld. Counsel for the appellant has further argued that in view of the fact that the plea of res­judicata had already been negatived by the Trial Court vide order dated 14.01.1998 and the said order having been upheld, there was no reason or occasion before the Ld. Trial Court to have allowed the application under Section 11 CPC without there being any pleadings of the Respondent No.1 to that effect and since there were no pleading before the Trial Court on the question of res­judicata, there was no issue in the suit on the said aspect. He has pointed out that it is settled law that where issues have been framed on merits, the judgment has to follow on all issues and not on one more particularly having regard to the fact that in the present case there was no issue with regard to the plea of res­judicata. He has in this regard placed his reliance on the authority of Smt. Sairindi Sahu & Anr. Vs. Pitabas Mahara & Ors. reported in 1992 (1) CLT 345 and argued that Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 7 of 22 issue relating to Res­judicata is not an issue of law and hence, cannot be treated as preliminary issue. (8) On the other hand Ld. Counsel for the respondent has vehemently argued that the appellant has no respect in law and legal process since by way of the present suit, the appellant has tried to re­agitate and reopen the issues which have already been put to rest by the Higher Courts including the Hon'ble Supreme Court in two rounds of litigation and hence under the given circumstances the present appeal filed against the order of the Ld. Trial Court is nothing but an abuse of the process of law and the appellant is guilty of contempt since even after loosing two rounds of litigation till the Hon'ble Supreme Court the appellant is continuing to waste the precious time of the Court. (9) I have gone through the Trial Court Record, the pleadings of the parties and the documents placed on record. I have also considered the written memorandum of arguments filed by the parties and the authorities relied upon by them which are as under: 1. In re Chunduru Venkata Subrahmanyam reported in AIR 1955 ANDHRA 74. 2. Shankar Lal Vs. Hira Lal reported in AIR (87) 1950 Privy Council 80. 3. Smt. Sarojini Rath Vs. Bhaskar Rath reported in AIR 1977Orissa 42. 4. A.M.K. Mariam Bibi & Ors. Vs. M.a. Abdul Rahim & Ors. reported in 2000 AIHC 661. Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 8 of 22 5. K. Laxminarasimha Chary Vs. K. Satyanarayana reported in 2002 AIHC 1478. 6. Alka Gupta Vs. Narender Kumar Gupta reported in AIR 2011 Supreme Court 9. 7. Delhi Development Authority Vs. Bhola Nath Sharma reported in AIR 2011 Supreme Court 428. (10) I have also gone through the orders passed by the various courts including the Delhi High Court and the Hon'ble Supreme Court. I may observe that the bare perusal of the impugned order of the Ld. Trial Court would show that the various aspects so highlighted by the parties and the judgments cited before it had been elaborately dealt with by the Ld. Trial Court while arriving at the conclusion that the suit of the appellant (plaintiff before the Ld. Trial Court) is barred by res­judicata and I quote the relevant paragraphs as under: ".......... 6. The Defendant filed an application under Section 11 CPC wherein it is prayed that this suit be dismissed as the suit filed by the present Defendant against the Plaintiff Sardar Darshan Singh has been decreed by the Court of Sh. Sanjeev Jain, CJ, Delhi, vide judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001. 7. This application is strongly opposed on behalf of Plaintiff and a detailed reply is filed in the year 2002, wherein it is submitted that appeal against the judgment dated 31.07.2001 has been filed and this judgment has not attend the finality. Thereafter, the present suit is being adjourned for one or other reasons including for the remedy opted by the parties in order to seek the relief in their favour in respect of suit disposed off vide judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001 before various Courts. 8. Following list gives the quick glance to the remedies opted by the parties and respective outcome:­ Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 9 of 22 Dated Event 31.7.2001 Judgment and decree is passed by the Court of Sh. Sanjeev Jain, CJ, Delhi in the Civil Suit bearing no. 347/2000/1981 titled as "Sardar Didar Singh VS Sardar Darshan Singh & Ors." filed for declaration and consequential relief of perpetual injunction, wherein following relief granted:­ "A decree for declaration with consequential relief of perpetual injunction in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant No.1 to the effect that by way of family partition/settlement the Plaintiff has become entitled to use the portion shown black in the site plan EX.PW­1/1 of the property no. F­18(AB), Moti Nagar, New Delhi in the same manner and with same rights and liabilities as Defendant No.1 has got vide lease deed and conveyance deed dated 31.08.1962 alongwith Defendants No.1 and 3 and restrain the Defendant No. 1 from selling, mortgaging, alienating, transferring or interfering in any manner in the above portion, except otherwise by due process of law" 24.12.2002 First appeal bearing no. RCA No.12/2001 filed by Sh. Darshan Singh titled as "Darshan Singh VS Didar Singh" against the judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001 passed by Sh.Sanjeev Jain Ld. Civil Judge, Delhi is dismissed with cost. 8.12.2005 Regular second appeal bearing no.11/2003 directed against order dated 24.12.2002 in RCA No.12/2001, whereby Ld. ADJ has upheld the judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001 was dismissed by Hon'ble High Court of Delhi. 7.4.2006 Special Leave Petition filed by Sh.Darshan Singh against the order dated 08.12.05 passed by Hon'ble High Court of Delhi is dismissed. Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 10 of 22 20.7.2006 Review Petition (C.No.564/2006) filed by Sh. Darshan Singh against order of Hon'ble Supreme Court passed in SLP is dismissed. 29.9.2009 Curative petition (C.No.29/2007) under review petition (C.No. 564/2006) in SLP (C.No.5625/2006) filed by Sardar Darshan Singh is dismissed. Certified copies of each and every order has been placed on record and the perusal leaves no doubt that the judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001 passed by Ld Court of Sh. Sanjeev Jain, then CJ, Delhi has achieved the finality. 9. It is strongly argued on behalf of Plaintiff that the present suit can not be dismissed by invoking the provision of Section 11 CPC on the following grounds:­ a. Explanation I of Section 11 CPC mandates that the judgment should be prior to the institution of the suit but the judgment and decree passed on 31.07.2001 is not prior in time to the date of filing of the present suit. b. Section 11 CPC does not bar the trial if issues have been framed. c. The specific plea for the dismissal of the suit under Section 11 CPC has to be prayed in the written statement by the Defendant but no such plea has been taken and it amounts to waiver. Ld. Counsel for the Plaintiff also relies upon the following judgment to substantiate its contention. 1. "K.Laxminarasimha Chary v.K.Satyanarayana" reported as 2002 AI HC 1478. 2. " Durga Prasad Sharma Vs. Sadasib Biswal " reported as AIR 1969 Orissa 171(V 56 C 58). 3. "Smt. Sairindi Sahu & Another Vs Pitabas Mahara & Others"(Civil Revision No.694 of 1990) reported as Orissa High Court. 4. " Smt. Sarojini Rath Vs. Bhaskar Rath"(Civil Revision No. 413 of 1975, D/­16.09.1976) reported as AIR 1977 Orissa 42 5. "A.M.K.Mariam Bibi & Others VS . M.A.Abdul Rahim & Others"(Appeal No.496 of 1985, D/­24.06.1999) reported as Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 11 of 22 2000 A I H C 661. 10. I have heard Ld Counsels and perused the record carefully. 11. Section 11 CPC provided as under:­ "11. Res judicata-- No Court shall try any suit or issue in which the matter directly and substantially in issue has been directly and substantially in issue in a former suit between the same parties, or between parties under whom they or any of them claim, litigating under the same title, in a Court competent to try such subsequent suit or the suit in which such issue has been subsequently raised, and has been heard and finally decided by such Court. Explanation I .-- The expression "former suit" shall denote a suit which has been decided prior to the suit in question whether or not it was instituted prior thereto. Explanation I leaves no doubt that the "former suit" within the meaning of Section 11 CPC is one which is decided prior to the suit in question irrespective of its date of institution. Therefore, the first contention raised on behalf of Plaintiff is contrary to the law and has no substance. So far as the second contention is concerned, I find such interpretation would be the violence to the intention of the legislation and literal construction of the provision. The doctrine of res­judicata are based on the principles that (1) no one should be vexed twice for the same cause and (2) the judicial order should be respected and allowed to be achieved finality so that contradictory or different decision may not be pronounced on the same facts and (3) there should be an end to the litigation. The factum of finality achieved by the judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001 passed by Sh. Sanjeev Jain then Ld. CJ, Delhi is established and admitted on behalf of Plaintiff. No trial is required to reach to this conclusion. Admission of fact may not be present into the pleading and it may be otherwise. The fact that the judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001 has achieved finality though disputed in the reply to the application under consideration, the same is admitted during the course of proceedings on behalf of the plaintiff as the same is revealed from the chronology of remedies opted. Therefore, I find no substance into 3rd contention raised on Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 12 of 22 behalf of Plaintiffs. The judgments relied upon by the plaintiff are perused. None of these lend any assistance to the contentions raised on behalf of the plaintiff and the same are not applicable to the present facts of the case. 12. Now only following points are to be considered by this Court to decide the application under Section 11 CPC as filed by the Defendants:­ a. Whether the two suits are between the same parties or between the parties under whom or any of them claimed? b. Whether the parties have been litigating under the same title? c. Whether the present suit presents the matter directly and substantially in issue which has been directly and substantially in issue in the former suit bearing no. 347/2000/1981? 13. Memo of the parties of the former suit bearing no. 347/2000/1981 presents following as a party:­ Sardar Didar Singh S/o Sh. Sant Singh R/o:­F­18, Moti Nagar, New Delhi­110015 .......................Plaintiff Vs 1. Sardar Darshan Singh S/o Sh.Sant Singh R/o:­F­18, Moti Nagar, New Delhi­110015 2. Sardar Sant Singh(Deceased) S/o Sh. Jaimal Singh R/o:­F­18, Moti Nagar, New Delhi­110015 3. Sardar Surinder Singh S/o Sh.Sant Singh R/o F­18, Moti Nagar, New Delhi­110015 4. Smt. Gurbachan Kaur W/o Sardar Gurbachan Singh R/o 2355/2498, Katra Madari Khan Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 13 of 22 Patel Nagar, Agra. 5. Union of India through its secretary, Ministry of Works, Housing & Rehabilitation New Delhi .........................Defendants 14. In brief, the former suit was filed by Sardar Didar Singh (the present Defendant) against the Sardar Darshan Singh and others on the basis of following:­ a. Sardar Sant Singh (the father of the parties in the present suit) was the displaced person from Pakistan and in lieu of his property in West Pakistan, he was granted compensation by rehabilitation department. He initially settled in Sidhipura and thereafter in Moti Nagar on allotment of property bearing no. F­18(AB), Moti Nagar, New Delhi. He got his claim and transferred the same in the name of Sardar Darshan Singh (the Plaintiff in the present suit) who is his eldest son, in order to get the property no. F­ 18(AB), Moti Nagar, New Delhi on perpetual lease. The entire investment for the purchase of lease hold right in this property was made by Sardar Sant Singh and this property was taken at the cost, instance and assistance of Sardar Sant Singh for the benefits of his sons on the assurance of Defendant No.1 (the plaintiff in the present suit) that he would give equal right and share to his younger brothers. b. In 1963, Sardar Sant Singh divided orally this property in 3 shares giving 1/3rd to each to his three sons (i.e. the plaintiff, defendant No.1 and 2 in the present suit). No family partition or settlement was executed/ registered but Sardar Didar Singh and one his brother Sardar Surinder Singh got the document of agreement to sale and Will executed from Sardar Darshan Singh and even Sardar Darshan Singh wrote to MCD for effecting the mutation of the portion in their respective name. Sardar Didar Singh raised construction over his portion at his own cost and he has in exclusive possession of the portion so given. This Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 14 of 22 property being on lease was not divisible as per the rules as such only mutation of house tax report was got effected but Sardar Darshan Singh wants to take benefits of lease deed/conveyance deed which is in exclusive name and he wants to sell the entire property, therefore the suit is filed by Sardar Didar Singh (the defendant in the present suit) praying for decree of declaration against Sardar Darshan Singh (the plaintiff in the present suit) to the effect that by way of family settlement/partition Sardar Didar Singh has become entitled to use portion of the property bearing no.F­18(AB), Moti Nagar, New Delhi in the same manner and for the same rights and liabilities as Sardar Darshan Singh has got vide lease deed and connivance deed dated 31.08.62 along with Sardar Darshan Singh and Sardar Surinder Singh and for perpetual injunction restraining him from selling, mortgaging, alienating, transferring or interfering in any manner. c. Sardar Darshan Singh contested this suit alleging that Sardar Surinder Singh has no interest or any claim in the suit property and Sardar Sant Singh did not get the suit property allotted in his name on the strength of his family or he was allowed on the strength of his family members. Sardar Darshan Singh claimed himself to be the absolute owner and lessee and it is denied that Sardar Didar Singh can co­equate himself to be the co­owner. 15. From the pleadings following issues were framed in the former suit bearing no.347/2000/1981:­ a. Whether the saving and earnings of the Plaintiff and Defendant No.1 to 4 were also invested in respect of the purchase of the property in suit? OPP. b. Whether there was any family partition between the parties as alleged in paras 9 & 10 of the plaint? If so to what effect? OPP. c. Whether the Plaintiff is entitled to the relief claimed? OPP. Note: This issue is amended vide order dated 30.09.1983. d. Whether the suit is bad by Section 80(2) CPC? OPD. Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 15 of 22 e. Whether the suit has not been valued for the purposes of Court Fee and jurisdiction? OPD. f. Whether the Court has no jurisdiction to try the suit? OPD. g. Whether the Defendant No.1 is the sole owner of the property in suit? OPD. h. Relief. 16. In order to decide the controversy in the present suit following needs the following adjudication:­ 1. Whether Sardar Darshan Singh is the sole owner of the property? (as framed in former suit) 2. Whether the defendants are co­owners of the property? (as framed in present suit). 3. Whether the defendants are licensee of plaintiff in respect of the suit property? OPD. If so, what effect. (as framed in present suit. Adjudication of first issue consequently decides the second and third issue as decision of first would result into negation of other. For the disposal of the former suit, issue regarding the sole ownership of the property by Sardar Darshan Singh was framed and it was adjudicated and decided against him. When this issue is decided against the Sardar Darshan Singh who is the Plaintiff in the present suit, it consequently decides that Sardar Didar Singh, the Defendant in the present suit is co­owner of the property. Thus, the relief of declaration is granted in favour of the present Defendant in the former suit. The decree in the former suit is passed thereby declaring that the present defendant No.1 is entitled to use the suit property in the same manner and with same rights and liabilities as the plaintiff in this suit had got vide lease deed and conveyance deed dated 31­08­1962 along with the present defendants. The relief and chronologically of the litigation show that no stone was remains untouched and this judgment and decree has been upheld and achieved finality. Therefore, this Court is of the considered opinion that not only parties are litigating under some title but the matter in the present suit has been directly and substantial judgment in the former suit and same has achieved the finality and trial of the present suit is Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 16 of 22 barred. 17. For these reasons, the application under Section 11 CPC is allowed and the present suit of the plaintiff is dismissed being barred by res­judicata......." (11) It is writ large that the Ld. Trial Court while deciding the issue of res­judicata has elaborately dealt with the previous litigations filed between the parties, the reliefs prayed for, the reliefs granted by the various courts and the orders passed therein, which litigations have not been disputed by the parties. The perusal of the judgments of the various courts confirm that there is no dispute in so far as the aspect of relationship between the parties is concerned. The following aspects emerge from the perusal of the Trial Court Record and the various documents placed before me: ➢ Late Sh. Sant Singh, father of Late Sh. Darshan Singh, Didar Singh and Surinder Singh was a displaced person from Pakistan and had left property in West Pakistan in lieu of which he was granted compensation by the Rehabilitation Department and Sh. Sant Singh was allotted the suit property bearing No. F­18­AB, Moti Nagar, New Delhi and Sh. Sant Singh had purchased other claims also and had paid the total consideration of the property. ➢ Sh. Sant Singh had taken the property for the benefit of all his three sons, although the Lease Deed/ Conveyance Deed was got executed by him in the name of his elder son Sh. Darshan Singh as the other two sons were minors at that time. Thereafter a Family Settlement had Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 17 of 22 taken place in the lifetime of Sh. Sant Singh and Sh. Darshan Singh had also executed documents including Agreement to Sell, Special Power of Attorney and Will in favour of the Respondent No.1 and Sh. Didar Singh and as per the respondents all the three brothers during the lifetime of their father had by way of oral partition, partitioned the suit property and further got mutated their respective shares in the suit property. ➢ After a passage of twenty years of settlement, the father of the Appellant developed cheating intentions and he wanted to deprive the Respondent No.1 and Sh. Didar Singh of their share in the suit property. ➢ Father of the appellant Sh. Didar Singh filed a suit for declaration and consequential relief on 23.12.1981 against Sh. Darshan Singh and the Respondent No.1 which suit was hotly contested by Late Sh. Darshan Singh and ultimately the suit was decreed in favour of Sh. Didar Singh vide judgment dated 31.07.2001 by the Court of Sh. Sanjiv Jain, then Civil Judge, Delhi, wherein it was held that the Respondent No.1 along with Sh. Didar Singh are entitled by way of Family Settlement to use the portion of the property in their possession in the same manner and with the same right and liabilities as Sh. Darshan Singh had got vide Lease Deed and Conveyance Deed dated 31.08.1962 and the relief of injunction was also granted in Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 18 of 22 favour of Sh. Didar Singh and against Late Sh. Darshan Singh. ➢ The father of the appellant challenged the judgment and decree dated 31.07.2001 before the First Appellate Court in RCA No.12/2001 which appeal was dismissed on 24.12.2002 by Sh. S.N. Aggarwal, the then ADJ, Delhi and RSA preferred before the Hon'ble High Court of Delhi bearing No. 11/2003 was dismissed on 08.12.2005. ➢ The Special Leave Petition bearing No. 5625/2006 was again dismissed by the Hon'ble Supreme Court on 07.04.2006 and a Review Petition bearing No. 564/2006 in SLP (C) No. 2625/2006 before the Hon'ble Supreme Court was also dismissed on 20.07.2006. A Curative Petition filed by Late Sh. Darshan Singh was also dismissed on 25.09.2007. ➢ Father of the appellant in the meantime had in the year 1995 filed a suit for possession separately against the Respondent No.1 and Sh. Didar Singh which was pending trial and after the death of Sh. Darshan Singh his legal heirs, the appellant and Respondent Nos.2 to 5 were impleaded as plaintiffs in the said suits. ➢ After the death of their father Sh. Satpal Singh and others i.e. the Respondent Nos. 2, 4 and 5 got a legal notice served on the Respondent No.1 and Sh. Didar Singh for vacating the suit premises and subsequently, filed a suit for declaration, possession, damages/ mesne profits against the Respondent No.1 and Sh. Didar Singh Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 19 of 22 wherein it was prayed that the judgment and decree passed by the Ld. Civil Judge in the suit titled Didar Singh Vs. Darshan Singh and which was upheld till the Hon'ble Supreme Court, be declared as null and void and decree for possession, damages/ mesne profits be passed in their favour as against the Defendant No.1 and 2 i.e. Didar Singh and Sh. Surinder Singh i.e. Respondent No.1 whereas the the appellant was made a performa defendant in the said suit. ➢ The Respondent No.1 along with Sh. Didar Singh filed an application under Order 7 Rule 11 read with Section 11 CPC which application was contested and ultimately was allowed by the Court of Sh. Rajiv Mehra, the then ADJ, Delhi on 23.10.2007 and the plaint was rejected. An appeal was filed against the said order before the Hon'ble High Court of Delhi which was dismissed on 02.12.2008 by the Division Bench comprising of Hon'ble Justice Pradeep Nandrajog and Justice J.R. Midha subject to the cost of Rs. 50,000/­. ➢ The SLP filed against the said order dated 2.12.2008 of Hon'ble Mr. Justice Pradeep Nandrajog and Hon'ble Mr. Justice J.R. Midha was dismissed in limine by the Hon'ble Supreme Court on 25.9.2007. ➢ Sh. Surinder Singh then filed an application under Section 11 read with Section 151 CPC in the suit for possession filed by Late Sh. Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 20 of 22 Darshan Singh (i.e. present suit) in which the appellant along with Respondent Nos.2 to 4 were impleaded as legal heirs of Late Sh. Darshan Singh which order is under challenge before this Court in RCA No. 70/2013. ➢ The Respondent No.1 along with Sh. Didar Singh had also filed a Writ Petition before the Hon'ble High Court of Delhi bearing No. WP (C) No. 248/2010 against the L&DO wherein it was prayed that the names of Surinder Singh and Sh. Didar Singh be also mutated in the Lease Deed/ Conveyance Deed. The said Writ Petition was allowed by the Court of Hon'ble Justice Murlidhar on 14.03.2010 and in compliance of the orders of the Hon'ble High Court, the name of the Respondent No.1 and Shri Didar Singh have also been incorporated in the Lease Deed by the L&DO. ➢ It was pursuant to the orders dated 14.03.2010 and 23.05.2010 passed by Hon'ble Mr. Justice Murlidhar that the names of Surinder Singh and Sh. Didar Singh were incorporated in the Lease Deed by the L&DO and a Mutation Letter dated 7.4.2010 was issued by the L&DO. (12) This being the background, I find no illegality in the order of the Ld. Trial Court warranting any interference holding that the suit of the appellant is barred under Section 11 CPC since the suit bearing No. 170/2007 was undisputedly between the same with the disputes relating to Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 21 of 22 same subject matter and similar relief of possession, damages/ mesne profits for illegal use and and occupation and consequential relief of injunction and hence the issues being directly and substantially the same. The fact that in the earlier round of litigation Sardar Didar Singh was held to be a co­owner of the property and hence in substance the relief of declaration already stands granted and cannot be reopened. It is writ large that the issues have already been finally put to rest after the orders of the Hon'ble Delhi High Court and the dismissal of the SLP by the Hon'ble Supreme Court. Earlier in the first round of litigation in Suit No. 592/1982 whose first appeal in RCA No. 12/2001 and second appeal in RSA No. 11/2003 were dismissed against which an SLP was filed and dismissed by the Hon'ble Supreme Court after which a Review petition was filed and dismissed thereafter. (13) After two full rounds of litigations there has to be some sanctity and finality attached to the proceedings and the verdict of the Highest Court needs to be respected and honoured. I find no merits in the appeal which is hereby dismissed. Trial Court Record be sent back along with the copy of this order. (14) Appeal File be consigned to Record Room. Announced in the open court (Dr. KAMINI LAU) Dated: 16.4.2015 ADJ­II(CENTRAL)/ DELHI Jaspal Singh Vs. Didar Singh, RCA No. 71/13 Page No. 22 of 22
